import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import re
import statsmodels.api as sm
import requests
from PIL import Image
from io import BytesIO
import cv2

Best category of videos for views¶

In [5]:
# To find best categorization of videos for more in depth analysis

# Fine-grained cutoffs from 0.5 to 200 minutes
cutoffs = np.arange(0.5, 200.01, 0.25)

cum_counts = [(channel_video_data['Duration_minutes'] <= c).sum() for c in cutoffs]

# Table
print("Cumulative Videos ≤ X minutes")
for c, count in zip(cutoffs, cum_counts):
    print(f"≤ {c:.2f} min  →  {count} videos")

# Plot - this will show the exact elbow
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 7))
plt.plot(cutoffs, cum_counts, marker='o', linewidth=2, color='#2E86C1')
plt.title('Cumulative Number of Videos by Duration Cutoff', fontsize=14, pad=20)
plt.xlabel('Duration Cutoff (minutes)', fontsize=12)
plt.ylabel('Number of Videos (Cumulative)', fontsize=12)
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

# Highlight common cutoffs
plt.axvline(x=1.0, color='red', linestyle='--', label='1.0 min (old rule)')
plt.axvline(x=1.5, color='orange', linestyle='--', label='1.5 min')
plt.axvline(x=2.0, color='green', linestyle='--', label='2.0 min')
plt.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Cumulative Videos ≤ X minutes
≤ 0.50 min  →  20 videos
≤ 0.75 min  →  69 videos

In [6]:
import numpy as np

# Create refined 4-category Video Type based on your plot
conditions = [
    (channel_video_data['Duration_minutes'] <= 1.75),
    (channel_video_data['Duration_minutes'] <= 30.0)
]

choices = ['Shorts', 'Medium']

channel_video_data['Video_Type'] = np.select(conditions, choices, default='Long')

# Check the distribution
print("=== NEW 4-CATEGORY VIDEO TYPE DISTRIBUTION ===")
print(channel_video_data['Video_Type'].value_counts().sort_index())

print("\nPercentage:")
print((channel_video_data['Video_Type'].value_counts(normalize=True) * 100).round(2))

=== NEW 4-CATEGORY VIDEO TYPE DISTRIBUTION ===
Video_Type
Long      149
Medium    529
Shorts    247
Name: count, dtype: int64

Percentage:
Video_Type
Medium    57.19
Shorts    26.70
Long      16.11
Name: proportion, dtype: float64
In [7]:
# ==========================
# 1. Overall Performance Snapshot
# ==========================
print("=== CHANNEL OVERVIEW ===")
print(f"Total Videos     : {len(channel_video_data):,}")
print(f"Total Views      : {channel_video_data['Views'].sum():,}")
print(f"Avg Views/Video  : {channel_video_data['Views'].mean():,.0f}")
print(f"Video Type Count   : {channel_video_data['Video_Type'].value_counts().to_string()}\n")

=== CHANNEL OVERVIEW ===
Total Videos     : 925
Total Views      : 7,304,454
Avg Views/Video  : 7,897
Video Type Count   : Video_Type
Medium    529
Shorts    247
Long      149
In [8]:
# ==========================
# 2. Video Type Performance (Most Important)
# ==========================
format_perf = channel_video_data.groupby('Video_Type').agg(
    Count=('Views', 'size'),
    Total_Views=('Views', 'sum'),
    Avg_Views=('Views', 'mean'),
    Avg_Likes=('Likes', 'mean'),
    Avg_Comments=('Comments', 'mean')
).round(0)

print("=== VIDEO TYPE PERFORMANCE ===")
print(format_perf)

=== VIDEO TYPE PERFORMANCE ===
            Count  Total_Views  Avg_Views  Avg_Likes  Avg_Comments
Video_Type                                                        
Long          149      1951774    13099.0      252.0          50.0
Medium        529      3946303     7460.0      244.0          44.0
Shorts        247      1406377     5694.0      123.0           5.0

Impact of upload timing on views¶

In [9]:
# === CREATE TIMING COLUMNS ===
channel_video_data['Published_date'] = pd.to_datetime(channel_video_data['Published_date'])
channel_video_data['Day_of_Week'] = channel_video_data['Published_date'].dt.day_name()
channel_video_data['Hour'] = channel_video_data['Published_date'].dt.hour

# === OVERALL BEST DAY & HOUR ===
timing_perf = channel_video_data.groupby(['Day_of_Week', 'Hour']).agg(
    Avg_Views=('Views', 'mean'),
    Count=('Views', 'size')
).round(0).reset_index()

# Best overall slots
best_slots = timing_perf.nlargest(10, 'Avg_Views')

print("=== TOP 10 BEST UPLOAD SLOTS (Highest Avg Views) ===")
print(best_slots[['Day_of_Week', 'Hour', 'Avg_Views', 'Count']])

=== TOP 10 BEST UPLOAD SLOTS (Highest Avg Views) ===
    Day_of_Week  Hour  Avg_Views  Count
101     Tuesday    11   126869.0      7
71       Sunday    19   112942.0      1
5        Friday     8    85985.0      8
49     Saturday    15    42824.0     22
66       Sunday    14    26809.0     16
58       Sunday     5    26756.0      1
44     Saturday    10    15468.0      3
38     Saturday     4    14281.0      1
124   Wednesday    18    14274.0      4
100     Tuesday    10    11596.0      5
In [10]:
# === FOR ENTIRE DATA ===
# === VISUALS ===
plt.figure(figsize=(14, 8))

# 1. Heatmap - Best Day × Hour
pivot = timing_perf.pivot(index='Day_of_Week', columns='Hour', values='Avg_Views')
day_order = ['Monday', 'Tuesday', 'Wednesday', 'Thursday', 'Friday', 'Saturday', 'Sunday']
pivot = pivot.reindex(day_order)

sns.heatmap(pivot, cmap='YlGnBu', annot=True, fmt='.0f', linewidths=0.5)
plt.title('Average Views by Day of Week & Hour of Upload\n(Darker = Higher Views)', fontsize=14, pad=20)
plt.xlabel('Hour of Day (24-hour format)')
plt.ylabel('Day of Week')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [11]:
# 2. Best Day Overall
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
day_avg = channel_video_data.groupby('Day_of_Week')['Views'].mean().reindex(day_order)
sns.barplot(x=day_avg.index, y=day_avg.values, palette='viridis')
plt.title('Average Views by Day of Week')
plt.ylabel('Average Views')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

C:\Users\PC\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_47732\2063116721.py:4: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(x=day_avg.index, y=day_avg.values, palette='viridis')
In [12]:
# ====================== 2. SEPARATE PLOTS FOR EACH VIDEO TYPE ======================
video_types = sorted(channel_video_data['Video_Type'].unique())

for vtype in video_types:
    print(f"\n🎯 {vtype.upper()} - Separate Analysis")

    subset = channel_video_data[channel_video_data['Video_Type'] == vtype]

    if len(subset) == 0:
        print(f"   No data for {vtype}")
        continue

    timing = subset.groupby(['Day_of_Week', 'Hour']).agg(
        Avg_Views=('Views', 'mean')
    ).round(0).reset_index()

    # Pivot for heatmap
    pivot = timing.pivot(index='Day_of_Week', columns='Hour', values='Avg_Views')
    pivot = pivot.reindex(day_order)

    # --- Heatmap for this type ---
    plt.figure(figsize=(16, 7))
    sns.heatmap(pivot, cmap='YlGnBu', annot=True, fmt='.0f', linewidths=0.5)
    plt.title(f'Average Views by Day & Hour - {vtype} Only', fontsize=14, pad=20)
    plt.xlabel('Hour of Day (24-hour)')
    plt.ylabel('Day of Week')
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    # --- Bar Plot for this type ---
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
    day_avg_type = subset.groupby('Day_of_Week')['Views'].mean().reindex(day_order)
    sns.barplot(x=day_avg_type.index, y=day_avg_type.values, palette='viridis')
    plt.title(f'Average Views by Day of Week - {vtype} Only')
    plt.ylabel('Average Views')
    plt.xticks(rotation=45)
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

🎯 LONG - Separate Analysis

C:\Users\PC\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_47732\658256046.py:33: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(x=day_avg_type.index, y=day_avg_type.values, palette='viridis')

Title Analysis¶

In [13]:
#====================== CREATE TITLE FEATURES ======================
data = channel_video_data.copy()

# Basic title metrics
data['Title_Length_Chars'] = data['Title'].str.len()
data['Title_Length_Words'] = data['Title'].str.split().str.len()

# Binary features
data['Has_Number'] = data['Title'].str.contains(r'\d', regex=True).astype(int)
data['Has_Question'] = data['Title'].str.contains(r'\?', regex=True).astype(int)
data['Has_Exclamation'] = data['Title'].str.contains(r'!', regex=True).astype(int)
data['Has_Emoji'] = data['Title'].str.contains(r'[\U0001F600-\U0001F64F\U0001F300-\U0001F5FF\U0001F680-\U0001F6FF\U0001F1E0-\U0001F1FF]', regex=True).astype(int)

# Engagement Rate
data['Engagement_Rate'] = (data['Likes'] + data['Comments']) / data['Views'].replace(0, np.nan)
data.head()
Out[13]:
Title Published_date Channel_Title Views Likes Dislikes Comments Definition Caption_Available Description ... Time_slot Day_of_Week Hour Title_Length_Chars Title_Length_Words Has_Number Has_Question Has_Exclamation Has_Emoji Engagement_Rate

4 From ₹10,000 Salary to ₹20+ Crores | Real Coac... 2026-03-26 14:12:06+00:00 Siddharth Rajsekar 1327 38 0 1 hd False From earning just ₹10,000 per month and facing... ... 2026-Mar Thursday 14 67 11 1 0 0 0 0.029390

5 rows × 25 columns

In [14]:
# ====================== VISUALS ======================
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))

# 1. Title Length vs Views (scatter)
plt.subplot(2, 2, 1)
sns.scatterplot(data=data, x='Title_Length_Words', y='Views', alpha=0.6)
plt.title('Title Length (Words) vs Views')
plt.xlabel('Title Length (Number of Words)')

# 2. Has Number vs Views
plt.subplot(2, 2, 2)
sns.barplot(data=data, x='Has_Number', y='Views', estimator=np.mean)
plt.title('Avg Views: Titles With vs Without Numbers')

# 3. Has Question vs Views
plt.subplot(2, 2, 3)
sns.barplot(data=data, x='Has_Question', y='Views', estimator=np.mean)
plt.title('Avg Views: Titles With vs Without Question Mark')

# 4. Engagement Rate vs Views
plt.subplot(2, 2, 4)
sns.scatterplot(data=data, x='Engagement_Rate', y='Views', alpha=0.6)
plt.title('Engagement Rate vs Total Views')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [15]:
# ANALYSIS 1: TITLE LENGTH vs VIEWS

print("=== ANALYSIS 1: TITLE LENGTH vs VIEWS ===")

length_perf = data.groupby(
    pd.cut(data['Title_Length_Words'], bins=[0, 5, 8, 12, 15, 20, 30])
).agg(
    Avg_Views=('Views', 'mean'),
    Count=('Views', 'size')
).round(0)

print(length_perf)

# Visual
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(data=length_perf.reset_index(), x='Title_Length_Words', y='Avg_Views', palette='viridis')
plt.title('Average Views by Title Length (Number of Words)')
plt.xlabel('Title Length (Words)')
plt.ylabel('Average Views')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.grid(axis='y', alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

=== ANALYSIS 1: TITLE LENGTH vs VIEWS ===
                    Avg_Views  Count
Title_Length_Words                  
(0, 5]                 4094.0     73
(5, 8]                 9561.0    300
(8, 12]                8266.0    440
(12, 15]               5047.0     84
(15, 20]               2736.0     28

C:\Users\PC\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_47732\741822768.py:16: FutureWarning: 

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(data=length_perf.reset_index(), x='Title_Length_Words', y='Avg_Views', palette='viridis')
In [16]:
# ANALYSIS 2: TITLE FEATURES PERFORMANCE

print("\n=== ANALYSIS 2: TITLE FEATURES PERFORMANCE ===")

features = ['Has_Number', 'Has_Question', 'Has_Exclamation', 'Has_Emoji']
feature_perf = []

for f in features:
    perf = data.groupby(f).agg(
        Avg_Views=('Views', 'mean'),
        Count=('Views', 'size'),
        Avg_Engagement_Rate=('Engagement_Rate', 'mean')
    ).round(2)
    feature_perf.append(perf)

feature_summary = pd.concat(feature_perf, keys=features)
print(feature_summary)

# Visuals (4 small plots)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(14, 10))
features_list = ['Has_Number', 'Has_Question', 'Has_Exclamation', 'Has_Emoji']
titles = ['Numbers in Title', 'Question Mark (?)', 'Exclamation (!)', 'Emoji in Title']

for i, ax in enumerate(axes.flat):
    sns.barplot(data=data, x=features_list[i], y='Views', estimator=np.mean, ax=ax, palette='Set2')
    ax.set_title(titles[i])
    ax.set_ylabel('Average Views')

plt.suptitle('Impact of Title Features on Average Views', fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

=== ANALYSIS 2: TITLE FEATURES PERFORMANCE ===
                            Avg_Views  Count  Avg_Engagement_Rate
                Has_Number                                       
Has_Number      0             8384.71    435                 0.05
                1             7463.48    490                 0.05
Has_Question    0             8390.35    842                 0.05
                1             2888.95     83                 0.06
Has_Exclamation 0             7845.12    827                 0.05
                1             8332.00     98                 0.06
Has_Emoji       0             7952.27    834                 0.05
                1             7387.52     91                 0.06

Passing `palette` without assigning `hue` is deprecated and will be removed in v0.14.0. Assign the `x` variable to `hue` and set `legend=False` for the same effect.

  sns.barplot(data=data, x=features_list[i], y='Views', estimator=np.mean, ax=ax, palette='Set2')
In [17]:
print("=== DYNAMIC DURATION TREND ANALYSIS (Line Graph) ===")

video_types = sorted(channel_video_data['Video_Type'].unique())

for vtype in video_types:
    subset = channel_video_data[channel_video_data['Video_Type'] == vtype].copy()

    if len(subset) < 10:   # Skip if too few videos
        print(f"Skipping {vtype} (too few videos)")
        continue

    print(f"🎯 {vtype.upper()} VIDEOS")

    min_dur = subset['Duration_minutes'].min()
    max_dur = subset['Duration_minutes'].max()

    # Dynamically decide number of bins (more bins = smoother line)
    num_bins = min(20, max(8, int((max_dur - min_dur) / 2)))   # adaptive

    subset['Duration_Bin'] = pd.cut(subset['Duration_minutes'],
                                   bins=num_bins,
                                   precision=2)

    # Calculate average views per bin
    trend = subset.groupby('Duration_Bin').agg(
        Avg_Views=('Views', 'mean'),
        Count=('Views', 'size')
    ).reset_index()

    # Get bin mid-point for clean x-axis
    trend['Duration_Mid'] = trend['Duration_Bin'].apply(lambda x: x.mid)

    # ==================== PLOT ====================
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))

    # Scatter of individual videos
    sns.scatterplot(data=subset, x='Duration_minutes', y='Views',
                    alpha=0.4, s=15, color='gray', label='Individual Videos')

    # Smooth trend line (Average Views)
    sns.lineplot(data=trend, x='Duration_Mid', y='Avg_Views',
                 linewidth=3, color='#FF6B6B', label='Average Views Trend')

    plt.title(f'Duration Sweet Spot Trend — {vtype} Videos\n(Smoother line = clearer peak)', fontsize=14)
    plt.xlabel('Video Duration (minutes)')
    plt.ylabel('Average Views')
    plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()

    # Print summary table
    print(trend[['Duration_Mid', 'Avg_Views', 'Count']].round(0))
    print("-" * 90)

Regression Analysis - WHAT DRIVES VIEWS THE MOST?¶

In [18]:
# === Check Distribution of Views ===
reg_data = channel_video_data.copy()
views = reg_data['Views'].dropna()

print("=== Views Distribution Analysis ===")
print(f"Total videos: {len(views)}")
print(f"Mean Views   : {views.mean():,.0f}")
print(f"Median Views : {views.median():,.0f}")
print(f"Max Views    : {views.max():,.0f}")
print(f"Skewness     : {views.skew():.4f}")

print("\nPercentiles:")
print(f"50th (Median) : {views.quantile(0.50):,.0f}")
print(f"75th          : {views.quantile(0.75):,.0f}")
print(f"90th          : {views.quantile(0.90):,.0f}")
print(f"95th          : {views.quantile(0.95):,.0f}")
print(f"99th          : {views.quantile(0.99):,.0f}")

# Rule of thumb for skewness
if views.skew() > 1:
    print("\n✅ Strongly right-skewed (typical for YouTube views)")
elif views.skew() > 0.5:
    print("\n⚠️ Moderately right-skewed")
else:
    print("\n❌ Not strongly right-skewed")

90th          : 9,198
95th          : 17,109

In [19]:
# If it is right-skewed (which it almost certainly is), you should use the log transformation on Views.

reg_data = channel_video_data.copy()

# Feature Engineering
reg_data['Title_Length_Words'] = reg_data['Title'].str.split().str.len()
reg_data['Hour'] = reg_data['Published_date'].dt.hour
reg_data['Day_Num'] = reg_data['Published_date'].dt.dayofweek

video_dummies = pd.get_dummies(reg_data['Video_Type'],
                               prefix='VideoType',
                               drop_first=True).astype(float)

# Build X (no Engagement_Rate)
X = pd.concat([
    reg_data[['Duration_minutes', 'Title_Length_Words', 'Hour', 'Day_Num']],
    video_dummies
], axis=1)

# Target with log transformation (best for skewed views)
y = np.log1p(reg_data['Views'])   # log(views + 1)

# Cleaning
X = X.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
X_clean = X.dropna()
y_clean = y.loc[X_clean.index]

X_clean = sm.add_constant(X_clean)

# Fit model
model = sm.OLS(y_clean, X_clean).fit()

print(model.summary())

# Feature Importance (Coefficients now show % impact on views)
importance = pd.DataFrame({
    'Feature': X_clean.columns,
    'Coefficient': model.params.round(4),
    'P_value': model.pvalues.round(4),
    'Significant': model.pvalues < 0.05
})

print("\n=== FEATURE IMPORTANCE (Sorted by Coefficient) ===")
print(importance.sort_values(by='Coefficient', ascending=False))

In [37]:
# If it is not right-skewed

# Fresh copy
reg_data = channel_video_data.copy()

# === Feature Engineering ===
reg_data['Title_Length_Words'] = reg_data['Title'].str.split().str.len()
reg_data['Hour'] = reg_data['Published_date'].dt.hour
reg_data['Day_Num'] = reg_data['Published_date'].dt.dayofweek

# Create dummies and convert bool → float (critical fix)
video_dummies = pd.get_dummies(reg_data['Video_Type'],
                               prefix='VideoType',
                               drop_first=True).astype(float)

# === Build X and y (WITHOUT Engagement_Rate) ===
X = pd.concat([
    reg_data[['Duration_minutes', 'Title_Length_Words', 'Hour', 'Day_Num']],
    video_dummies
], axis=1)

y = reg_data['Views'].copy()

# ====================== CLEANING ======================
print("=== Data Types Check ===")
print(X.dtypes)

# Convert everything to numeric (extra safety)
X = X.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')

# Drop any remaining NaN rows
print(f"\nRows before dropping NaNs: {len(X)}")
X_clean = X.dropna()
y_clean = y.loc[X_clean.index].copy()
print(f"Rows after dropping NaNs: {len(X_clean)}")

# Add constant for intercept
X_clean = sm.add_constant(X_clean)

# ====================== RUN OLS REGRESSION ======================
model = sm.OLS(y_clean, X_clean).fit()

print("\n" + "="*80)
print(model.summary())

# ====================== FEATURE IMPORTANCE ======================
importance = pd.DataFrame({
    'Feature': X_clean.columns,
    'Coefficient': model.params.round(4),
    'P_value': model.pvalues.round(4),
    'Significant (p<0.05)': model.pvalues < 0.05
})

print("\n=== FEATURE IMPORTANCE (Sorted by Coefficient) ===")
print(importance.sort_values(by='Coefficient', ascending=False))

Thumbnail features checking¶

latest 925 Shorts...
In [21]:
def analyze_thumbnail(thumbnail_url):
    """
    Analyzes YouTube thumbnail with:
    - Basic visual features
    - Text coverage estimation
    - Face detection
    """
    try:
        # Download thumbnail
        response = requests.get(thumbnail_url, timeout=15)
        response.raise_for_status()

        img = Image.open(BytesIO(response.content)).convert('RGB')
        img_array = np.array(img)

        # Resize for faster processing
        if img_array.shape[0] > 800:
            scale = 800 / img_array.shape[0]
            img = img.resize((int(img_array.shape[1] * scale), 800), Image.LANCZOS)
            img_array = np.array(img)

        height, width = img_array.shape[:2]
        total_pixels = height * width

        # Convert to grayscale
        gray = cv2.cvtColor(img_array, cv2.COLOR_RGB2GRAY)

        # === Basic Visual Features ===
        brightness = float(np.mean(gray))
        contrast = float(np.std(gray))

        # Colorfulness
        r, g, b = [img_array[:,:,i].astype(float) for i in range(3)]
        rg = np.abs(r - g)
        yb = np.abs(0.5 * (r + g) - b)
        colorfulness = float(np.sqrt(np.mean(rg)**2 + np.std(rg)**2 +
                                    np.mean(yb)**2 + np.std(yb)**2))

        # Sharpness
        laplacian = cv2.Laplacian(gray, cv2.CV_64F)
        sharpness = float(np.var(laplacian))

        # === Text Coverage Estimation ===
        blurred = cv2.GaussianBlur(gray, (5, 5), 0)
        thresh = cv2.adaptiveThreshold(blurred, 255, cv2.ADAPTIVE_THRESH_GAUSSIAN_C,
                                       cv2.THRESH_BINARY_INV, 11, 2)
        kernel = cv2.getStructuringElement(cv2.MORPH_RECT, (3, 3))
        dilated = cv2.dilate(thresh, kernel, iterations=2)

        contours, _ = cv2.findContours(dilated, cv2.RETR_EXTERNAL, cv2.CHAIN_APPROX_SIMPLE)

        text_pixels = 0
        for cnt in contours:
            area = cv2.contourArea(cnt)
            if 50 < area < (total_pixels * 0.15):
                x, y, w, h = cv2.boundingRect(cnt)
                aspect_ratio = w / h
                if 0.2 < aspect_ratio < 8.0:
                    text_pixels += area

        text_coverage_percent = (text_pixels / total_pixels) * 100 if total_pixels > 0 else 0.0

        # === Face Detection ===
        # Load pre-trained face detector (Haar Cascade - comes with OpenCV)
        face_cascade = cv2.CascadeClassifier(cv2.data.haarcascades + 'haarcascade_frontalface_default.xml')

        # Detect faces (scaleFactor and minNeighbors tuned for thumbnails)
        faces = face_cascade.detectMultiScale(
            gray,
            scaleFactor=1.1,
            minNeighbors=5,
            minSize=(30, 30)
        )

        face_count = len(faces)
        face_pixels = 0

        for (x, y, w, h) in faces:
            face_pixels += w * h

        face_coverage_percent = (face_pixels / total_pixels) * 100 if total_pixels > 0 else 0.0
        has_face = 1 if face_count > 0 else 0

        return {
            'thumb_brightness': round(brightness, 2),
            'thumb_contrast': round(contrast, 2),
            'thumb_colorfulness': round(colorfulness, 2),
            'thumb_sharpness': round(sharpness, 2),
            'text_coverage_percent': round(text_coverage_percent, 2),
            'face_count': int(face_count),
            'face_coverage_percent': round(face_coverage_percent, 2),
            'has_face': int(has_face)
        }

    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Failed to process {thumbnail_url}: {str(e)}")
        return {
            'thumb_brightness': np.nan,
            'thumb_contrast': np.nan,
            'thumb_colorfulness': np.nan,
            'thumb_sharpness': np.nan,
            'text_coverage_percent': np.nan,
            'face_count': np.nan,
            'face_coverage_percent': np.nan,
            'has_face': np.nan
        }


# ====================== Example Usage ======================

# Test with one thumbnail
url = "https://i.ytimg.com/vi/uxiU2YiqJo8/hqdefault.jpg"   # Replace with your own
result = analyze_thumbnail(url)
print(result)

{'thumb_brightness': 62.93, 'thumb_contrast': 76.96, 'thumb_colorfulness': 80.74, 'thumb_sharpness': 2868.24, 'text_coverage_percent': 0.6, 'face_count': 1, 'face_coverage_percent': np.float64(1.12), 'has_face': 1}

# Create Video Type dummies (though all are Shorts, keeping it for consistency)
video_dummies = pd.get_dummies(reg_data['Video_Type'], prefix='VideoType', drop_first=True).astype(float)

# ====================== BUILD X and y ======================
X = pd.concat([
    reg_data[['Duration_minutes', 'Title_Length_Words', 'Hour', 'Day_Num',
              'thumb_brightness', 'thumb_contrast', 'thumb_colorfulness',
              'thumb_sharpness', 'text_coverage_percent',
              'face_count', 'face_coverage_percent', 'has_face']],
    video_dummies
], axis=1)

y = np.log1p(reg_data['Views'])        # log transformation (recommended)

# ====================== CLEANING ======================
X = X.apply(pd.to_numeric, errors='coerce')
X_clean = X.dropna()
y_clean = y.loc[X_clean.index]

print(f"Final sample size after cleaning: {len(X_clean)} (out of) {len(X)}")

X_clean = sm.add_constant(X_clean)

# ====================== RUN REGRESSION ======================
model = sm.OLS(y_clean, X_clean).fit()

print("\n" + "="*70)
print("OLS REGRESSION RESULTS - Latest 100 Shorts")
print("="*70)
print(model.summary())

# Feature Importance
importance = pd.DataFrame({
    'Feature': X_clean.columns,
    'Coefficient': model.params.round(4),
    'P_value': model.pvalues.round(4),
    'Significant': model.pvalues < 0.05
})

print("\n=== FEATURE IMPORTANCE (Sorted by Coefficient) ===")
print(importance.sort_values(by='Coefficient', ascending=False))
